edges.description: Defective fibrillin microfibrils fail to properly sequester latent TGF-beta complexes.
Loss of normal fibrillin scaffold destabilizes elastic fiber architecture and ECM integrity.
Altered fibrillin-integrin interactions impair mechanosensing in load-bearing connective tissues.
Excess SMAD signaling increases protease activity and maladaptive ECM turnover.
Persistent TGF-beta activity accelerates medial degeneration and progressive root dilation.
Dysregulated growth factor signaling contributes to long bone overgrowth.
Abnormal connective tissue growth signaling contributes to elongated digits.
Elastic fiber fragmentation and collagen disarray weaken the aortic wall.
Connective tissue degeneration in valve leaflets promotes prolapse.
Weak zonular microfibrils predispose to lens displacement.
Ligamentous laxity and vertebral connective tissue weakness promote spinal curvature.
Fragile connective tissue in lung apices increases risk of spontaneous pneumothorax.
Maladaptive vascular smooth muscle responses to pulsatile stress accelerate root enlargement.
Inflammatory ECM damage and smooth muscle dysfunction increase risk of acute intimal tearing.
Oxidative injury amplifies wall fragility and susceptibility to dissection in advanced disease.
Progressive dilation weakens the aortic wall, predisposing to acute dissection.

nodes.description: Progressive dilation of the aortic root beginning in childhood, with risk of life-threatening aortic dissection.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiovascular surgery in Marfan syndrome: implications of new molecular concepts in thoracic aortic disease.
evidence.snippet: MFS is one of the most common inherited connective tissue disorders affecting 1 in 5000 individuals. Although the phenotype of MFS can be quite variable, aneurysmal dilation of the aortic root and consecutive acute aortic dissection is the leading cause of death in this patient population.

evidence.reference_title: Early Onset Marfan Syndrome with multivalvular insufficiency: Report from a tertiary hospital in Tanzania, and a review of the recurrent c.7606G>A p.0 variant in FBN1.
evidence.snippet: Early Onset Marfan Syndrome is at the severe end of the Marfan syndrome spectrum and is frequently associated with variants in exons 24-32 of the FBN1 gene.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical presentation and echocardiographic findings of Thai patients with Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The majority of those individuals had significant organ involvement including dilatation of ascending aorta (78%).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical significance of family history and bicuspid aortic valve in children and young adult patients with Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder of the connective tissue, whose cardinal features affect eyes, musculoskeletal, and cardiovascular system.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiovascular molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: The relation between Marfan syndrome and fibrillin mutations and that between supravalvular aortic stenosis and William syndromes and elastin mutations are reviewed.

evidence.reference_title: Marfan syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0004942 | Aortic aneurysm | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Life-threatening tear in the aortic wall allowing blood to enter between tissue layers, the leading cause of death in untreated Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiovascular surgery in Marfan syndrome: implications of new molecular concepts in thoracic aortic disease.
evidence.snippet: MFS is one of the most common inherited connective tissue disorders affecting 1 in 5000 individuals. Although the phenotype of MFS can be quite variable, aneurysmal dilation of the aortic root and consecutive acute aortic dissection is the leading cause of death in this patient population.

evidence.reference_title: Determinants of rapid progression of aortic root dilatation and complications in Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Aortic dissection occurred more frequently in group R (7 patients, 47%) than in group S (0%, P < 0.001).

evidence.snippet: HP:0002647 | Aortic dissection | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Progressive enlargement of the aortic root diameter, typically measured by echocardiography and tracked using Z-scores adjusted for age and body surface area.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002616 | Aortic root aneurysm | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Abnormally long, slender fingers and toes (spider-like digits), often demonstrated by positive wrist and thumb signs.

evidence.reference_title: Arachnodactyly represented in art.
evidence.snippet: Arachnodactyly, a term used since 1902 to describe abnormally long (spider-like) fingers, is a pathologic feature of several heritable conditions, notably the Marfan syndrome...

evidence.reference_title: Arachnodactyly--a key to diagnosing heritable disorders of connective tissue.
evidence.snippet: Arachnodactyly literally means spidery fingers, and describes the long, slender fingers typical of patients with Marfan syndrome (MFS).

evidence.reference_title: Marfan syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001166 | Arachnodactyly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Defective fibrillin-1 microfibrils fail to sequester latent TGF-beta complexes, leading to increased bioavailable TGF-beta and excessive SMAD2/3 signaling that drives vascular smooth muscle cell phenotypic switching and ECM remodeling.

biological_processes: transforming growth factor beta receptor signaling pathway

nodes.description: Superior and temporal displacement of the lens due to weakened zonular fibers, affecting visual acuity.

evidence.reference_title: Marfan's Syndrome with Ectopia Lentis.
evidence.snippet: Marfan's Syndrome with Ectopia Lentis.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular findings in 87 adults with Ghent-1 verified Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: In this strictly defined MFS group fulfilling the Ghent-1 criteria, the prevalence of EL was 62.1%.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of zonular diseases.
evidence.snippet: The mechanisms implicated in the clinical manifestations of zonular diseases, especially ectopia lentis, are reviewed.

evidence.reference_title: Current concepts of ocular manifestations in Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome is a widespread disorder of connective tissue. It is characterized by systemic and ocular features due to mutations in the fibrillin gene.

evidence.reference_title: Management of ectopia lentis in children.
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome, homocystinuria, trauma, and simple ectopia lentis are the most common causes of pediatric lens subluxation.

evidence.reference_title: Ectopia lentis.
evidence.snippet: Ectopia lentis may cause a marked reduction in visual acuity, which varies with the type and degree of dislocation and the presence of other ocular abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic findings in Chinese families with congenital ectopia lentis.
evidence.snippet: Congenital ectopia lentis (EL) refers to the congenital dysplasia or weakness of the lens suspensory ligament, resulting in an abnormal position of the crystalline lens, which can appear as isolated EL or as an ocular manifestation of a syndrome, such as the Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Uncovering the Hidden World of Aqueous Humor Proteins for Discovery of Biomarkers for Marfan Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Ectopia lentis is a hallmark of Marfan syndrome (MFS), a genetic connective tissue disorder affecting 1/5000 to 1/10 000 individuals worldwide.

evidence.reference_title: Marfan syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001083 | Ectopia lentis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, elastic fiber fragmentation, altered collagen composition, and proteoglycan accumulation (especially versican) weaken the aortic wall medial layer.

evidence.reference_title: FBN1 gene mutations in 26 Hungarian patients with suspected Marfan syndrome or related fibrillinopathies.
evidence.snippet: FBN1 gene mutations lead to MFS and related connective tissue disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Impact of pathogenic FBN1 variant types on the development of severe scoliosis in patients with Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We retrospectively evaluated 278 patients with pathogenic or likely pathogenic FBN1 variants.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic FBN1 Genetic Variation and Aortic Dissection in Patients With Marfan Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Aortic risk has not been evaluated in patients with Marfan syndrome and documented pathogenic variants in the FBN1 gene.

evidence.reference_title: The fibrillinopathies: New insights with focus on the paradigm of opposing phenotypes for both FBN1 and FBN2.
evidence.snippet: Different pathogenic variants in the fibrillin-1 gene (FBN1) cause Marfan syndrome and acromelic dysplasias.

evidence.reference_title: [Ocular manifestations of Marfan syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome is an orphan disease that is caused by a mutation in the FBN1 gene located on chromosome 15 (15q21.1).

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant Marfan syndrome caused by a previously reported recessive FBN1 variant.
evidence.snippet: Pathogenic variants in FBN1 cause autosomal dominant Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Compound-heterozygous Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We report two families in which the probands have compound-heterozygous Marfan syndrome (MFS).

evidence.reference_title: FBN1 / Marfan syndrome (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: FBN1 | HGNC:3603 | Marfan syndrome | MONDO:0007947 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Mutations in the FBN1 gene result in defective fibrillin-1 protein, which affects the elasticity and strength of connective tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of defects in the fibrillin gene and protein in individuals with the Marfan syndrome and related disorders.
evidence.snippet: The gene encoding for fibrillin, the FBN1 gene on chromosome 15, has been identified as the defective gene causing the Marfan syndrome. Fibrillin is the large glycoprotein with a repetitive domain structure and is a major protein component of microfibrils, a fibrillar system closely associated with elastin in connective tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Generation of Marfan syndrome-specific induced pluripotent stem cells harboring FBN1 mutations.
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome (MFS) is a hereditary condition caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene. Genetic mutations in the FBN1 locus impact the function of the encoded protein, Fibrillin 1, a structural molecule forming microfibrils found in the connective tissue.

evidence.reference_title: The importance of mutation detection in Marfan syndrome and Marfan-related disorders: report of 193 FBN1 mutations.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the FBN1 gene have been characterised in patients affected by Marfan syndrome and Marfan-related disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Update of the UMD-FBN1 mutation database and creation of an FBN1 polymorphism database.
evidence.snippet: Fibrillin is the major component of extracellular microfibrils. Mutations in the fibrillin gene on chromosome 15 (FBN1) were first described in the heritable connective disorder, Marfan syndrome (MFS).

evidence.reference_title: [Marfan syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome is a genetic disorder of the connective tissue caused by mutations in FBN1 gene.

evidence.reference_title: [Ocular manifestations of Marfan syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Marfan syndrome is an orphan disease that is caused by a mutation in the FBN1 gene located on chromosome 15 (15q21.1) and is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.

evidence.reference_title: C596G mutation in FBN1 causes Marfan syndrome with exotropia in a Chinese family.
evidence.snippet: A C596G mutation in FBN1 was identified in a Chinese family with MFS. Our results expand the spectrum of FBN1 mutations and contribute to the understanding of the role of FBN1 in the pathogenesis of Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Aortic asprosin overexpression does not ameliorate disease pathophysiology in a murine model of Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Together, these findings indicate that asprosin overexpression does not ameliorate the aneurysmal phenotype in MFS.

nodes.description: Perturbed integrin-fibrillin interactions alter focal adhesion signaling and mechanosensing, contributing to maladaptive vascular smooth muscle cell responses to hemodynamic stress.

Emerging evidence implicates mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in vascular smooth muscle cells and endothelial cells as contributors to thoracic aortic aneurysm progression in Marfan syndrome.

biological_processes: mitochondrial ATP synthesis coupled electron transport

nodes.description: Myxomatous degeneration of mitral valve leaflets causing prolapse into the left atrium during systole, often accompanied by mitral regurgitation.

nodes.description: Lateral curvature of the spine occurring in approximately 63% of Marfan syndrome patients, often progressive.

evidence.reference_title: Infantile scoliosis in Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The data on all patients seen at one institution who had Marfan syndrome and scoliosis by age three were reviewed.

evidence.reference_title: Analysis of GPR126 polymorphisms and their relationship with scoliosis in Marfan syndrome and Marfan-like syndrome in Mexican patients.
evidence.snippet: A large cross-sectional study revealed a 63% prevalence of scoliosis among patients with MFS.

evidence.reference_title: Impact of pathogenic FBN1 variant types on the development of severe scoliosis in patients with Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Among the several musculoskeletal manifestations in patients with Marfan syndrome, spinal deformity causes pain and respiratory impairment and is a great hindrance to patients' daily activities.

evidence.reference_title: Musculoskeletal diseases in Marfan syndrome: a nationwide registry study.
evidence.snippet: One out of six with Marfan syndrome was registered with scoliosis (HR: 36.7 (27.5-48.9).

nodes.description: Spontaneous lung collapse occurring in 5-11% of patients due to apical blebs from connective tissue abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: [Recurrent spontaneous pneumothorax revealing Marfan's syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Though cardiovascular, skeletal and ophthalmological manifestations are the most frequent features, the respiratory system can also be involved.

evidence.reference_title: [Respiratory manifestations of Marfan's syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Marfan's syndrome is a rare genetic disorder...but minor diagnostic criteria also include pulmonary manifestations. Pneumothorax, frequently relapsing, affects 5 to 11% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Optimal treatment of pneumothorax in adolescents with Marfan syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Pneumothorax often develops in patients with Marfan syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Pneumothorax and bullae in Marfan syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Boys and girls from the MFS group were significantly taller than those in the non-MFS group and in the reference group at all ages (P<0.0001).

evidence.snippet: Early Onset Marfan Syndrome is at the severe end of the Marfan syndrome spectrum and is frequently associated with variants in exons 24-32 of the FBN1 gene... presented with tall stature.
